label: Ch1 P1
text:
Chapter 1: Finding My Path
Look, before we dive into this whole journey, I need to be crystal clear about something: this book isn't your blueprint. It's not "10 Easy Steps to Crypto Millions" or "Copy My Trades and Get Rich." If that's what you're looking for, close this book right now and go find some YouTube guru who'll promise you Lambos by next Tuesday.
This is the story of how I found MY path through the absolute chaos of crypto markets, from broke designer to eight figures and back down again. More importantly, it's about how understanding my own story, my psychology, my strengths, my spectacular failures, became the foundation for sustainable success.
Why am I telling you this? Because the most valuable thing you'll get from this book isn't my strategy. It's the realization that you need to build your own. Every successful investor I know has a completely different approach, shaped by their unique experiences, skills, and psychological makeup. The only thing we have in common? We stopped trying to be someone else and started being ruthlessly honest about who we are.
So yeah, I'll tell you about the Squirrel Strategy and how I turned zero into millions. But more importantly, I'll show you how to excavate your own wisdom from your own experiences. Because that's the only strategy that actually works when shit gets real.
With that out of the way, let's get into how a kid with average grades stumbled into crypto and found his purpose...
The Unconventional Foundation
Life's a trip when you look back on it. The path to where I am now wasn't planned out or anything. It's been a series of random choices, accidents, obsessions, and learning to see opportunities nobody else noticed.
Back in school, I was honestly pretty average. Not the kid teachers expected big things from. The traditional education system with all its memorization bullshit and conformity just didn't work for me. While I wasn't crushing standardized tests, I was learning in my own way, mostly stuff they don't teach you in classrooms, stuff that required digging and genuine curiosity.
My dad was hardcore. Self-made entrepreneur in computers who pushed me constantly to explore new hobbies and get out of my comfort zone. Sometimes it felt like he was being a dick, but looking back, he was building resilience in me that would save my ass later. My mom was the opposite, total support system, always believing in whatever crazy idea I had next. Having both those influences shaped me more than I realized.

label: Ch1 P2
text:
support system, always believing in whatever crazy idea I had next. Having both those influences shaped me more than I realized.
But here's the thing that would prove crucial decades later: at 14, I started what I called my annual "hero's journey", systematic consciousness exploration through psychedelics that served as a psychological reset and growth accelerator. This wasn't recreational drug use. From the beginning, I approached these experiences as training, deliberate practice for developing psychological capacities that I somehow knew I'd need later, even though I couldn't have articulated what for.
Every year, I'd undertake this journey to check myself, dissolve accumulated ego patterns, and maintain connection to deeper perspectives that ordinary consciousness often loses. It was like reorganizing a hard drive, clearing out the psychological debris that accumulates from living in consensus reality.
What I didn't know then was that this consciousness work was developing several key psychological capacities that would prove essential for crypto success: comfort with uncertainty, unwavering conviction without rigidity, emotional regulation under extreme stress, and long-term vision amidst short-term chaos. These weren't just abstract benefits, they would translate into specific advantages that compounded over time.
The System Skeptic Awakens
The big wake-up call came during university when I did a deep dive on the 2008-2009 subprime mortgage crisis. Seeing how the entire financial system nearly collapsed because of greed, complexity, and straight-up fraud changed something in me. I remember thinking, "This whole system is broken, and most people have no idea how it really works."
That experience in university was huge for me. Aside from building my network at York University, understanding how busted the financial system was became my biggest takeaway from university. It planted the seed of skepticism, the need to question everything, which is vital for any squirrel trying to navigate a forest full of predators.
This wasn't just academic curiosity. The 2008 crisis revealed something fundamental: traditional systems that everyone took for granted could fail catastrophically. Most people learned to be more careful within the existing system. I learned to look for alternatives to the system itself.
